segment-text: PRODUCTION WHITE - 9/29/21 EXT. THE CLOUDS

White. The CAMERA TILTS DOWN to SEE: a handful of some of the tallest older BUILDINGS in NEW YORK CITY. We begin to PAN, AWAY FROM THE SKYLINE...THROUGH MIST... Across the BRIDGE and the RIVER...toward... QUEENS. And all those residential houses, and low-level buildings...
INT. PUBLIC SCHOOL CLASSROOM - MORNING
SUPERIMPOSE THE LEGEND: "QUEENS, NEW YORK. FALL, 1980."
A public school: P.S. 173Q. A large SIXTH GRADE classroom.

The desks and books and blackboard are worn, perhaps as much as 30 years old; cursive handwriting templates line the top of the room and are yellowing with antiquity. The classroom is INSANELY PACKED AND FILLED WITH THE ENERGY OF FORTY-FIVE CHILDREN. It is a real mix of ethnicities: white, Black, Asian, Latino. Tall, short. Fat, skinny. In short, everyone looks different from everyone else. The TEACHER, a diminutive man named MR. TURKELTAUB, is at the blackboard. He CLAPS HIS HANDS REPEATEDLY for ATTENTION:
Arright, come on, settle down!
The CLASS QUIETS. A beat. He begins:
Welcome to first day of school. My name is Mr. Turkeltaub, and I hope you all had a good summer. Mine, on the other hand, was a little too short, but what else is new.

As Turkeltaub speaks, the CAMERA MOVES IN, not TO A CLOSE SHOT ON THE TEACHER, but rather to a CLOSE SHOT on: A DRAWING, on a desk, over the shoulder of a student. Primitive, but not bad: a rendering of MR. TURKELTAUB, drawn in pencil, on white lined paper, in one of those black-withwhite speckled "composition" books so popular with grade school. REVEAL the "artist," a boy of twelve: PAUL GRAFF, red-haired and pale and freckled and bespectacled. He PLANTS his TONGUE in his cheek wall as he works. Over this image:
CUT BACK to ANGLE ON TURKELTAUB as he looks at his book:
Okay then...let's go. Jennifer
Here!

A KID'S VOICE ANGLE BACK ON PAUL, who looks around to see if anyone is looking. No one is. So with a smirk, he holds his drawing up to his classmates. Many of the boys eye his masterpiece with curiosity as Mr. Turkeltaub continues to look at his attendee list. (The GIRLS ARE MUCH MORE DILIGENT AND ORDERLY, far better behaved.) A NOTE is passed to PAUL: "DRAW A TURKEY BODY!" MR. TURKELTAUB
Here!
ANOTHER KID'S VOICE PAUL DRAWS a TURKEY BODY on Turkeltaub's portrait, then SHOWS the DRAWING AGAIN. LAUGHTER. TURKELTAUB LOOKS UP. A BEAT OF SILENCE. Nothing. Back to the book:
Jonathan Davis?
In the BACK OF THE CLASSROOM: JOHNNY DAVIS, tall, Black, a year older than the other kids. He grins and stands:
(bad English accent) The name's Bond--JAMES Bond!
GOLDENROD REVISIONS (11/17/21)
The CLASS LAUGHS, and Paul turns to Johnny, smiles in approval. Another kid snatches the drawing, passes it AROUND.
Mr. Davis! You and I are old friends at this point, and you KNOW I will NOT tolerate any nonsense! (death stare, then)
Here...
KID'S VOICE Turkeltaub LOOKS UP, sees Paul's drawing being passed about.
Arright, give me that! Come on-YOU!
Paul panics. A fellow student sheepishly hands the drawing to the teacher. Turkeltaub looks at it.
Who did this? Silence.
Who DID this?!?
If no one admits to it, there's no gym. Who did this drawing? (silence) Okay. I'm counting to three, and then there's no gym for a week. (silence) (silence)
Paul STANDS. Doesn't say anything. CLASSMATES GIGGLE. Mr. Turkeltaub looks at his attendance book, walks over to him.
What's your name?
Paul Graff. Tears well up in Paul's eyes. Turkeltaub looks tenderly at
him for a moment, then thinks better of it:
And you think this is appropriate? (beat) I'm ASKING you a question.
I...just wanted to make everyone laugh.
Oh, a comedian. You wanna be Mr. Popularity, is that it? Okay, stand up there, in front.
PAUL WALKS TO THE FRONT OF THE ROOM. a mistake on the teacher's name: A KID'S VOICE calls out
Mr. Turkey--Turkeltaub? How many loose-leafs will we need?
JOHNNY DAVIS stands, FLAPS his ARMS like a CHICKEN. and THE CLASS, BREAK INTO HYSTERICAL LAUGHTER.
JONATHAN DAVIS! Get down here!
Davis points to his own chest.
Yes, you! I'd think after last year you might've grown up a little, but I guess that'd be too much to ask!
Turkeltaub grabs a desk, spins it violently to the front of the room right near the blackboard. Davis takes his things and makes his way to the FRONT OF THE CLASSROOM.
No wonder it's your second time through sixth grade with me! 'Cause you got nothing up here, Mister--NOTHING!
Turkeltaub points to his HEAD.
(under his breath) Animal...

Davis has heard this, and the insult lands. He is humiliated, and sits in a solo desk right in front of the blackboard. Paul FACES the blackboard, standing right next to the seated JOHNNY DAVIS. Then, A SOUND of CHIMES: it comes from a very old loudspeaker above the blackboard. A VOICE: WOMAN'S VOICE (LOUDSPEAKER FUZZ)
for the Pledge of Allegiance of the
Someone makes a "RASPBERRY" NOISE:
HEY! HEY! SHUT IT, RIGHT NOW!
INT. CLASSROOM - LATER

Mr. Turkeltaub is trying to teach a math class. Paul is WASHING DOWN THE BLACKBOARD on the SIDE OF THE CLASSROOM. Davis pounds erasers. Turkeltaub is writing on the front blackboard, speaks aloud his writing:
Exchange...one ten...for ten ones...
He turns around to the class and repeats it, like a mantra. Meanwhile, EDGAR ROMANELLI, a classmate, raises his hand:
Exchange one ten for ten ones! And that's how you subtract large
I don't understand, Mr. Turkeytaub.
Turkletaub! I just told you, Mister--?
Edgar Romanelli.
MISTER ROMANELLI. Were you listening? Repeat it to yourself. It's very clear if you LISTEN! Class? EXCHANGE ONE TEN FOR TEN

EXCHANGE ONE TEN FOR TEN ONES! Every time Turkeltaub turns to the blackboard in front, his back to the class, PAUL does a DISCO TRAVOLTA MOVE. The CLASS CLOWN, for sure. LAUGHTER. Turkeltaub, his back still to the classroom:
Mr. Davis, I have eyes in the back
I didn't do anything!
Cut it out or you go to Principal
Sebell's office!
We SEE a FLASH of GUILT across Paul's FACE; he LOOKS at Johnny.
INT. CLASSROOM - LATER
An ellipse. The class is learning cursive. Paul stands in front of the blackboard. Turkeltaub hovers over students as they write:
A "G" and an "S" aren't the same letter... (checks the clock)
Okay, all of you need to sign your permission slips. For a trip, next week--to the Guggenheim Museum. Now line up, against the wall,
shortest to tallest-The class utters a collective "YESSSS!" Johnny gets up, starts to walk to the door--as does Paul.

No, not you, not the two of you. Gym is a privilege. (to Johnny) Especially not you. Sit back down. And when you hear my whistle for end of gym, out there, THAT'S when you can go home. (to the rest of the class) All right, let's go! Double file!

The CLASS DEPARTS, except for JOHNNY DAVIS and PAUL GRAFF. MOMENTS LATER: ANGLE THROUGH WINDOW The STRAINS of DISCO music. PAUL WATCHES as the class is spread out in the SCHOOLYARD, in LINES. They are throwing beanbags in the air and catching them. After a while, we SEE/HEAR TURKELTAUB SHOUTING through an electric bullhorn:
And...FREEZE!
Turkeltaub BLOWS HIS WHISTLE. Everyone STOPS throwing beanbags and FREEZES. Idiotic "exercise." Paul looks over to Johnny, who is sorting through small white CARDS.
Yeah?
JOHNNY DAVIS
I woulda, um, said something if you really got in trouble.
(shrugs; then) Don't matter. (MORE)
Turkey acts like he could see behind him--like he's got special powers and shit. But he don't ever do nothing.
What're you looking at...?
Apollo mission patch stickers.
Oh wow, that's so cool...
My step-brother give them to me. He's in the Air Force, down in Florida. (beat; re: outside) Turkey's still playing that disco shit.
(a laugh) I know. And disco like, totally sucks.
You know Kurtis Blow, Graff? Sugar Hill Gang?
No, not really... But I have a lot of records at home.
What d'you got?
I have...the Beatles' red and blue albums? Which is basically all their best songs. They might get back together soon, I heard. No reaction really from Davis, so:
You could borrow 'em if you want. You got a record player? No.
But I know somebody that does.
Then, A WHISTLE from BELOW/OUTSIDE, and TURKELTAUB'S VOICE:
And, FREEZE! ARRIGHT! ENOUGH!
(snort; then) He don't ever give the full period.
ANGLE ON THE YARD as it empties.
Davis and Graff move to
EXT. P.S. 173Q/"FRESH MEADOW LANE" - MOMENTS LATER
Paul and Johnny are walking away from the school toward a desolate street. They look at the Apollo Mission Patch stickers:
Cape Kennedy sounds so cool...with all the rockets, and palm trees, and everything.
Definitely. And the astronauts live down there, too. (beat) I could do that--join the Air Force and be a pilot, then go to astronaut school.
Yeah...that'd be amazing.
It's a lot of training, though. You gotta be like, super sharp.
(nods; then) I--I like to build rockets. I'm gonna get a model of the Saturn V-the moon rocket. (beat) C'I see?
(as he hands it over) I gotta be careful. I don't want Turkey to take 'em, like he took your drawing.
I know! But my mother's President of the P.T.A., and I bet she could get him in a lotta trouble.
Oh, THAT I wanna see. Turkey shitting hisself would be excellent.
Beat. They arrive at a bench. stickers in his backpack. Johnny sits, putting his
You going on the school trip?
(shrugs; then) Dunno. Costs a lot.
I could probably get the money for you--my family's pretty rich. We went to England with my grandpa last year, and we saw Big Ben. Cool.
(beat; smiles) So you could just get your mother to sign and it'd totally be party time!
Actually I stay with my grandma. And she don't remember nothing. Sometimes she don't even remember me.
(awkward laugh) That's so weird... Where you live again?
Hollis. (beat; gesturing) Gotta get on the bus. Cool hanging with you, Graff. See you tomorrow.
JOHNNY DOES A SLOPPY MILITARY SALUTE:
Paul smiles, salutes, too. The two walk separate ways.
EXT. PAUL'S HOUSE

A semi-attached row house in a working class neighborhood in Flushing, Queens. Paul walks up the steps, takes out a preposterously long keychain, tries to open the door. It won't stretch long enough. He has to unlatch it, not an ideal system.
INT. PAUL'S HOUSE - VESTIBULE
Paul enters through the small vestibule leading to the living room. Silence. Paul calls up. Ted?
Dad?
Excited, to himself, with a fist pump: YESSSSS.
He walks up the stairs.
INT. PAUL'S HOUSE - PARENTS' ROOM

Paul enters his parents' room. Ambles over to a chest below a large mirror. He goes through his mother's jewelry. Paul looks at a wedding picture of his father and mother, which is inside the box. They look happy. HE LIFTS the FELT DRAWER, under which he finds a stack of CASH. He takes TWO TWENTY DOLLAR BILLS and pockets them. Checks himself in the mirror in front of him. Aloud: Shadowboxes.
Turkey! Hey TURKEY!!! "I'm heavyweight champion of the world!"
Mocks a CHEERING CROWD, does a ROCKY-STYLE KNOCKOUT PUNCH.
INT. PAUL'S BEDROOM

THE CAMERA PANS AROUND: a Reggie Jackson poster and a Muhammad Ali poster and a COMIC COVER on the wall. Toy Soldier WALLPAPER. Model planes hang from the ceiling. He is DRAWING a SUPERHERO, sitting with his knees down on the carpeted floor, his ass up in the air. HE HEARS SOMETHING: JANGLING KEYS. THE CLICK OF A DOOR LOCK. PAUL'S FACE LIGHTS UP. A HUGE GRIN ON PAUL'S FACE.
INT. LIVING ROOM

Paul's Grandfather, GRANDPA AARON RABINOWITZ, enters with a grocery bag (marked "King Kullen"). He wears a polyester shirt and slacks, and a Greek fisherman's cap. Smiles broadly. Paul HUGS the man tightly and kisses him:
Hey Grandpa!
Hello there, young man! Mmmm, give you a hugga-mugga.
Check THIS baby out. I made my own superhero: "Captain United!"
Paul shows him his latest drawing:
What's that, a new one... Ah... He's flying, high up over the city. (keeps looking) 'S very impressive.
(precocious, with humor) Thank you, my good man!
GRANDPA AARON RABINOWITZ pulls from his coat pocket a pack of
Hey.
I got you some jellybeans.
He throws the pack to PAUL, who catches it.
GOLDENROD REVISIONS (11/17/21)
Mom says I can't eat them--they're bad for my teeth.
Ah, get out of here. I been eating jellybeans all my life and look at my teeth--perfect.
Rabinowitz OPENS the VESTIBULE COAT CLOSET to hang his coat. It makes a LOUD SQUEAK and FEELS QUITE STIFF.
(to himself) What's with this, here... Grandpa?
Mmm?
(preoccupied)
I think I want to be a famous artist when I grow up.
Good! You can do whatever you want to do. But if you're gonna be famous, you've got to sign the drawing. Right? All the great artists, they sign their work.
Oh yeah, I forgot. (looks at shopping bag) What's for dinner?
Let's take a look--uh--look what I
see-Grandpa SHOWS him the bag's contents: RONZONI and RAGU. CLAPS, then throws his hands up in TRIUMPH:
Oh yeah! Now THAT is what I call a DINN--NNAY!
Hooray, for spaghetti! You know, I never had spaghetti when I was your age. You're a lucky kid.
Oh my God, NEVER? What'sa wronga witha spaghetta la sauce?
What's wrong is my mother thought it was bloody worms. (back to the door hinge) Today was your first day of school, I heard.
Yes indeedy. I hung out with my friend Johnny. And we're going on a trip next week, to the Guggenheim Museum.
Very nice...
Paul dives into another bag: an ESTES SATURN V model rocket:
Holy cow!!! You got it, that's amazing! Let's make it!
Just the first few steps today-everyone'll be here for dinner
soon. And oop-He grabs Paul's arm gently:
Don't tell your mother--it'll be our secret.

Paul nods, hugs his grandfather around the waist. The background is the DINING ROOM, dark. The CAMERA DOLLIES PAST THEM and towards the DARK ROOM. THE LIGHT COMES UP in the DINING ROOM and the SUN GOES DOWN. TIME HAS PASSED:
THE DINING ROOM TABLE,

Now set. A woman, ESTHER GRAFF (more on her in a moment), enters with platters in her hands. She places the food on the table. Counts place settings.. A SCREAM, over an image of the HAMSTER in CLASPED HANDS. WOMAN'S VOICE (O.C.)
It's a RAT, in the house!!! WIDEN TO REVEAL:
INT. PAUL'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - LATER

Paul's IMMEDIATE family. That would be: his brother, TED, three years older, DRESSED IN PREP SCHOOL ATTIRE. Holding the hamster. Also here: his grandmother, MIRIAM (also known as "MICKEY"); his great aunt RUTH, 70s, and his great uncle LOUIS, late 60s, otherwise known as LOBBY and a dead ringer for Jimmy Durante.
(laughing at her horror) It's a hamster, Aunt Ruth. Named Rosie. For my science project.
GET IT THE HELL OUTTA HERE!
Ruthie, it's just the kids' pet!
We had enough of them growing up, Mickey! Get it AWAY from me!

Laughing, Ted puts his hamster into a small cage. Paul and his Grandfather come downstairs. AD-LIBBED "HELLO"s. PAUL IS BEAMING, holding part of the MODEL ROCKET. Grandma looks at Aaron, vaguely accusatory:
Where'd you two wander off to?
(sheepish) Upstairs...working on a little project for him, that's all.
Ted approaches Paul:
What's the project, shithead?
'S for school.
You mean Fake School--you actually have to work at MY school. And get real punishment. But you're probably too dumb to get in!
With a shit-eating grin, Ted punches Paul in the arm. OWWW!
Why'd you do that?
'Cause I felt like it.
INT. KITCHEN

ESTHER GRAFF at the stove. IRVING, her husband and Paul's father, PULLS HIMSELF OUT FROM BEHIND THE REFRIGERATOR. IRVING, age 42, is dressed in a V-neck T-Shirt and dark gray pants; he's a strange mix: Stanley Kowalski with a PhD. Paul's mother, ESTHER, is earthy, dark-haired. Rolls her facial tissue in her sleeve.
I think I figured it out why it's
making that grinding sound. The
Okay, but I need you away from there at some point. I need to get to my food this month.
Aaron enters, sees the refrigerator away from the wall.
What's happening in here? 'S not working? Hi Dad.
I don't know the story.
Aaron then turns, puts on an apron which has distinctly feminine touches.
Aaron--before you do that, maybe you could help me with this. I mean, if anyone could, you could!
Let's see what we got...
Aaron bends down, does a voltage meter reading. As they do, Esther steps over them, opens the fridge. Irving starts to laugh; re Grandpa:
(smiles gently) Looks all right.
Irving stands up; he grabs a piece of salami.
INT. LIVING ROOM
The boys are fighting. Aunt Ruth:
C'mere! This is what your great grandparents were like! You should look through this every once in awhile!
Ruthie, they don't need to see that. Come on.
They should learn about the family.

The TWO BOYS SETTLE DOWN A BIT, look OVER the SHOULDERS of the seated Aunt Ruth and Uncle Lobby. Grandma Mickey disengages, intentionally. She watches with dismay. ANGLE ON PAUL as they eye a coffee table book: "THE OLD COUNTRY," by Abraham Schulman. Leafing through: PICTURES of EASTERN EUROPEAN JEWS, c. 1900: Entering the living room: Irving, who is now eating the piece of salami and belching. He SEES the boys. To Paul:
Why don't you and your brother get out your instruments. And play.
Without argument, Paul goes to the piano.
No, please, Dad.
I suck.
Well, if you learn to play better, you'll become, you watch--you'll be the most popular person at any party.
By playing the concertina!?
Yes, by playing your concertina, wise guy! It's better than playing a, a, a bunga-bunga instrument! (beat) G'head. For your grandparents. Be a nice thing to do.
GOLDENROD REVISIONS (11/17/21)
Ted stands next to Paul, picks up his concertina. Irving STANDS by the piano, "conducting." The two boys unhappily play possibly the worst rendition of a TBD Public Domain song in all human history.
It's so hard...wait...
GOLDENROD REVISIONS (11/17/21)
The grandparents try to get into it, despite the fact that both kids are terrible at their instruments:
Isn't that absolutely marvelous! Beautiful boys!
She summons them, like the Dowager Empress, and they both kiss her on the cheek. She is royalty. Ted then PINGS Paul behind the EAR: OWWWWWW!
Frustrated, Paul slams his hand down on the piano. goes to the kitchen: As he
Where you going? 'Was terrific!
INT. KITCHEN

Where Esther is slaving over the stove. The local news is on the television, and Grandpa Aaron divides his attention between the television (local news--"Live at Five," with Jack Cafferty and Pia Lindstrom) and his kitchen duties.
Ma, Ted pinged my ear! No response. MA.
I heard you... So?
He pings you and you ping him. The two of you just need to stop. Because I can't take it anymore.
What do you mean? HE. PINGED ME!
And you're totally innocent, I'm sure.
YES!!!!
I AM!!!
I don't believe you, I'm sorry.
Unexpectedly, she SMILES at him, STICKS OUT HER TONGUE:
How was school, buster?
It was fine... I have Mr. Turkeltaub. Can you sign a permission slip? We have a class trip next week-(claps hands together once) Into the City! With my frey-hends! [Meaning "friends"]
(with a playful shimmy; pointing) Your mother did it already, at my school meeting.
Grandpa hands Paul a slice of cucumber, which he makes DISAPPEAR; then he makes it reappear in his other hand. Amused, Paul takes it. As Paul eats it, chomping loudly:
Ma, how much power do you have at school? Do you run everything?
Hearing this, Grandpa LAUGHS. So does Esther.
No, I don't run everything. I'm president of the P.T.A. Why?
Just wondering. (re the food; derisive) What is this? Scrod.
It's a type of fish.
Whoa, I'm definitely not eating that.
Why not? Here, look--it flakes. That's how you know it's fresh--
Like fresh DOGGY do. I'll just eat the spaghetti and order dumplings from Fan Fan, for delivery.
No, you're NOT gonna order dumplings. We HAVE food here.
We'll soon see about that.
NO. Don't you dare, buster! I'm not paying for it--you think we're millionaires here?!?
As Paul LEAVES the kitchen, his mother infuriated, she tries a different tactic:
I love you!
I don't care!
INT. DINING ROOM
The family around the table. A dinner. In medias res.
(summoning courage) So. I have decided. With all your support, I'm gonna try and run for District School Board. (beat; no reaction, so:) Okay?
At first, Irving doesn't respond, then belches, making an "OOOH-pah" SOUND. Finally:
You gonna win?
With your support, I will.
Oop, sounds like you're not gonna!
(to Irving) Did you hear what he just said??? (back to Paul) I said I needed your SUPPORT. Not rude comments.
Don't make yourself objectionable for once. Okay?
(SIMULTANEOUS) (almost under his breath) Be nice.
You're gonna catch your father's temper.
Esther, why on earth would you run for the board, with Ted in private school?
For Paul's sake! To have a say!
I'm sorry, I think that's absolutely crazy. Paul should be going to Teddy's school anyway. And we can help. Can't we, Aaron?
Aaron does not answer. Paul PERKS UP. CONCERN on HIS FACE:
He's fine where he is for now, Mom.
(nose wrinkling) I'm just saying...the public school system is just not what it was when I taught there.
Grandma!? I like school--all my friends're there.
Aaron looks at Paul's plate, then at Paul, who eats only the spaghetti.
You'll make friends anywhere. (to Esther) The class sizes are out of control, and the kids that they have coming in from the neighborhoods from all over. The Blacks, coming in... MA.
They have nowhere else to go, Mickey. Their own schools are falling apart.
ACROSS THE TABLE, AUTH RUTH, TO TED, SIMULTANEOUS, looking at a GLASS on the table and speaking a tad too loudly:
I don't believe it, these are my
glasses. I brought them back from
YOU were in Czechoslovakia, Aunt Ruth? Isn't that Communist?
(as if he should know) I worked for the Pentagon, right after the war! Relocating displaced persons, in Prague!
That's so cool!
Subversively, Grandpa Aaron PUTS more pasta on Paul's plate. Paul grins widely at his grandfather in gratitude.
Your Great Aunt is a very impressive person.

I went to a thrift store there and found all these glass kiddish cups. I was gonna bring them home to my father. But I saw names at the bottom of the cups--and I knew they were stolen from Jews who went to the camps! I almost fell over.
SIMULTANEOUS: Irving COUGHS; an ENORMOUS BALLOON of COFFEE starts to BLOW OUT HIS NOSE as his eyes slam shut. Only Paul and Ted see it and start LAUGHING HYSTERICALLY. Paul points right at Irving.
How could you kids laugh about that!? The Nazis took them, to the camps! (to Esther) (MORE)
Esther, the kids're laughing, about Nazis!
MORE LAUGHTER from the boys. The adults are uncomprehending. Esther looks to her father, who shrugs it off. Irving turns, looks at Paul's FULL PLATE:
(to Paul, re his plate) Behave. And eat the meat, that's all. Here-Irving pushes some of the fish towards Paul, separating it
from the bone. Aaron has been pushing the carbs, and tries to behave himself.
I don't see why we can't just have Chinese food every night.

Because then we wouldn't have a single dollar for other things. I teach HOME ECONOMICS. You know what that means? That means I teach how to stick to a BUDGET. (to Irving) He doesn't know the meaning of the dollar. Or how HARD we work. Does. Not. Get. It. At all.
Paul lets out a "SQUAWK" sound to imitate his mother. Everyone ignores it. As Paul gets up from the table, he snaps his fingers, grins, looks at everyone:
Dumplings, suckers!
Ted PICKS UP the dish and, grinning, CAUSES the SPAGHETTI TO SLIDE ON THE DISH. To PAUL:
Get me some fried!
Now YOU don't like dinner?
The spaghetti has all this water in it, Ma. It's slipping and sliding on the dish.
Don't play games with that.
(to Paul) Where are you going? Don't you dare.
"Hey Mamma, you're a tourist trap, Hey Mamma, your pasta tastes like crap..."
(to Ted) Stop. You heard your grandfather, put that down! You'll wind up with it all over the rug!
IRVING EYES TED WITH INTENSITY.
INT. KITCHEN
Where Paul picks up the telephone. Starts to dial a number.
Put down the phone! (Paul does not) I said, put down the phone! (to her husband)
(still eyeing Ted) Paul, put down the phone! No Ching Chang Cho food tonight! Stop, your mother made dinner!
You're not ordering dumplings! We have plenty of food here, food I slaved over! Now PUT DOWN THE PHONE!
PAUL! Hang up the phone!
INT. DINING ROOM
Uncle Louis sits, eating everything in sight as Ted rotates his plate. With a shit-eating grin:
Uncle Louie, which horses did you bet on today? Did you win?
Shhhh! C'mon, kid, don't say nothing!
INT. KITCHEN
Paul on the phone:
Hello, I'd like to place an order for delivery--one order, fried dumplings, and one order roast pork fried rice.
INT. DINING ROOM
Esther STANDS AND SCREAMS to NO ONE IN PARTICULAR:

HE'S ORDERING CHINESE FOOD--I DON'T BELIEVE IT! I HONESTLY DON'T BELIEVE IT!!!! The DINNER FALLS APART COMPLETELY, with Ted SINGING AGAIN and holding his plate in a circular fashion--and the spaghetti SLIDING OFF HIS PLATE and onto the FLOOR.
GODDAMNIT!!! WHAT DID I SAY?!?
Oh boy--that'll stain! Jeez! You got club soda?!? Quick!
IRVING is DARK, his JAW CLENCHED. HE TAKES HIS BELT OUT OF HIS PANTS and LOOPS IT, as if to SWAT TED. As TED RUNS AWAY:
I'm gonna RAP you one!
INT. BATHROOM - LATER

Paul comes out of the bath. He rubs the towel across his back, violently, then crouches in front of a small electric heater. He YELLS so that the whole house--if not the whole neighborhood--can hear: Grandpa?!?
Get into bed already or you're gonna get the "bastinado!"
He'll be up in a minute! Brush your teeth!
Paul walks over to the sink, wets the toothbrush, then puts it back in the rack without brushing.
INT. KITCHEN

Esther cleans up, with Grandpa Aaron's help, near the sink. The Mets game is playing in the background, on a small green transistor radio the shape of a ball. At the kitchen table, with coffee: Irving, Ruth, Mickey. Watching the "McNeil/Lehrer News Hour" on PBS.
If you're losing feeling in your leg, then you need to get it checked.
Yeah. I will... It'll all be a lotta nothing.
Let's hope. (louder, to the table) Miriam, d'you want the Chinese leftovers? Paul's dinner?
No no, don't give 'em away--I'LL eat them!
(beat) I'm tired...
Aaron senses his daughter's flagging composure:
He starts dancing with her; her spirits revive. RONALD REAGAN on the television: A clip of
"If we let this be another Sodom and Gomorrah... Maybe we might be the generation that sees Armageddon..."
(re Reagan; shrugs) Sounds like a Class-A schmuck.
INT. BEDROOM

The room is lit mostly from the hallway. Paul looks through the book: "The Old Country." BLACK AND WHITE PHOTOS of miserable-looking and impoverished souls. A silhouette appears in the doorway, and Paul slides the book down the side of his bed.

In the car!!! NOW!!!! your mouth shut!

He IS SEATED NEXT to JOHNNY DAVIS. NEITHER SAYS A WORD TO THE OTHER. But...Johnny is cuffed, and he is NOT... The CAR PULLS AWAY.

Paul is accompanied to what looks like a small cage. He looks around; no one is paying attention to him. It is MADNESS in here. Where did they take Johnny? THEY ARE INSPECTING VOTING MACHINES into the FRONT PART of the STATION. The POLICE SERGEANT D'ARIENZO is DIRECTING WORKERS:
The polling areas'll be in front-all the voting machines go in front, guys! (to himself) Goddamned election, can't wait 'til it's over with already.
At last, the SERGEANT approaches, and they open the CAGE.
INT. STATION - NEAR THE DESK
JOHNNY IS HERE, at the opposite end of the table. OFFICER PATRICK SCOTT leans to D'Arienzo, who has entered. In his ear:
Pawn shop owner called it in...hot serial number on the unit...
D'Arienzo listens, then sits down, talks to Paul.
Do you know this young man?
Paul nods, petrified.
My friend.
PAUL GRAFF
Well, your friend's in real trouble here. He stole a computer. Pregnant pause from the cop.
Wanna tell me about that?
I...I don't really know what happened...
Then what were you doing there? You just said he's your friend--how good do you know him? (beat) The two of you're in real trouble. A beat.
(re Paul) He didn't do nothing. It's me.
Paul is STUNNED by this. SPEECHLESS.
Oh, he was just along for the ride? (to Paul) You're a little angel? Your friend did it all? Um...
I don't...
Blub blub blub, you got no words all of a sudden.
He didn't do it.
(finally) No--that's--that's not true. What's that?
We both did it. (beat) From where I go to school.
All I know is, you guys're now telling verrry different stories here.
I swear! (beat) We were trying to get enough money to go to Florida... No!
The Sergeant's face sours at the absurdity of the "plan."
Look, whatever is going on at home, if you have a real problem, there are people you can talk to here.
I don't have any problems at home. I just hate it.
He's just saying some shit. didn't do anything.
Oh my God, why are you saying that? (closer, sotto) Why are you telling them that?!?
He wasn't even in there. (turns to Paul) Right?
Shut up now, the both of you. We're gonna have to sort these little fairy tales out later.
D'Arienzo is distracted by another office entering, whispering in his ear. D'Arienzo leaves.
(whispers, to Johnny) Why are you telling them that?
Angrily, Johnny gives him a visual sign, gritting his teeth, as if to say: "SHUT YOUR MOUTH, DON'T FUCK THIS UP."
NO ANSWER. MOMENTS LATER, IRVING ARRIVES. is filled with fear. He SEES Paul, who Johnny is watching the whole thing that follows. The SERGEANT SEES IRVING. A look comes over him, a broad SMILE:
Yes...?
Irving Graff? IRVING GRAFF
The Sergeant WARMLY sticks his hand out to shake:
Tom D'Arienzo, I live over on Jewel Avenue. We got a mutual friend, Nick Bloom?
Oh yes, yes, hello!
AWKWARD BEAT.
So my son here is uh...involved in something?

SERGEANT D'ARIENZO says nothing. Thinks for a moment. Then he motions for Irving to ENTER an OFFICE, which he does. The two have a CONVERSATION, and Paul watches through GLASS... Then Paul TURNS back to JOHNNY. PATRICK SCOTT sits down and tries to act friendly to Johnny. Dulcet but forced tones:
Sit tight, we're just gonna get someone to talk with you. But we're all your friends here. Arright?
Johnny sits there, staring at Scott for a moment. he PIPES UP, equal parts contempt and heartbreak:
You're not my friend. (beat) And you're not ever gonna be my friend. (beat) You're gonna tell me some shit, you care about this and that. (MORE)
But I'm not gonna listen to that shit from any of you. 'Cause you don't care, and you're never gonna care. There ain't no one else gonna stick up for me except me.
Johnny turns, LOOKS AT PAUL. After a BEAT, the men EMERGE.
(to Paul; quietly) Come with me.
Paul doesn't move. Shocked.
Listen to your father.
Let it go, man. Don't make no difference. (beat) Let it go.
I said, come ON.

EXT. PAUL'S HOUSE - STREET - MOMENTS LATER
The Plymouth Station Wagon pulls up to the house.
INT. FAMILY CAR. NIGHT
Paul is inside. IRVING is behind the wheel. Gives a weary EXHALE. After SILENCE:

Please can we just go into the house, Dad? You don't have to hit me. I won't get into trouble again, I promise-IRVING GRAFF I'm not gonna hit you, Paul. But we got very, very lucky. That's all I'm gonna tell you.
Paul does not answer. Parks the car.
I fixed that cop's hot water heater a few years ago, and I didn't charge him. That's why you're not up shit's creek right now, in a juvenile detention center somewhere.
Dad--I have to tell you something. What. I did it.
I did--
(cutting him off) Everybody makes mistakes. won't do it again.
What's going to happen to my friend?
(shrugs, shakes his head) You're a Black and you get a police record, you're in deep shit. My guess is you never see him again.
It wasn't his fault-IRVING GRAFF (forcefully, almost enraged) Shut your mouth. Okay?

Paul stares at his father, then looks down, devastated. Irving calms, grows ever more introspective. In fact, at moments, his expression contorts, as though he is conflicted inside, torn apart, devastated:
Life is unfair. Nobody likes that-it's the worst thing in the world. But I learned long time ago--be thankful when you're on the winning side. We're Jews, usually we aren't. (MORE)
When you get older you can change the world. Right now, you just need to get past this and become a mensch.
A long BEAT. Paul LISTENS, without sarcasm, with humility. Irving changes gears, becomes more parental:

Your friend got the shaft, you feel bad. I understand that. And in a lotta ways, you'll never be closer to your friends than when you're this age. So I'm sure you'll miss him. But there's a time when you just have to put being a kid behind you. Maybe it's too early for you, I dunno. But this is your time, and you can't choose. You may feel you let him down, and I won't lie to you: it may haunt you the rest of your life. Trust me, I know. So all you can do is now make the most of your break, and don't look back. Do you hear what I'm saying to you? Yes.
(sotto)
TEARS BEGIN TO FORM IN IRVING'S EYES. gently touching his son's cheek: He LOOKS at his son,
Let's go inside, please. (beat) You need to just forget about all this. Let's hope it doesn't break your mother's heart. She's already having a rough enough time lately.

The TWO ENTER the dark house. Irving goes toward the kitchen, Paul walks up the stairs slowly, as though in a horror film. OVER THIS, we HEAR:

The ENTIRE FAMILY, HUDDLED AROUND the TELEVISION. IRVING slices a PICKLED TOMATO. ESTHER is a bit in her own world, folding clothes on the kitchen table...

Paul ambles over to the kitchen table. SEES THE STACK OF CLOTHES. He takes one of the articles of clothing--

Paul enters his room. Opens his attache case, starts taking out his books. He STARES for a MOMENT in the GLASS of a PICTURE ON HIS WALL.

IN THE REFLECTION, GRANDFATHER AARON, SITTING IN A CHAIR!!!!! GRANDPA AARON seems to be arranging his toy CAR COLLECTION in orderly fashion.

THE FOREST MANOR TEACHERS and STUDENTS. The F-M students are boisterous, irrepressible. Paul is among them. A MAN'S VOICE. IT IS FRED TRUMP. Paul listens with focus:

Happy Thanksgiving tomorrow, first of all. It was always my favorite holiday--but not because of the food, mind you. No, I remember the good time, the family, the friends. And this is a wonderful tradition we have, that our family has sponsored over the years. The
